scenario:
Alfa Numerio Limited is a Public Listed Company listed on National Stock Exchange. The Board of Directors consists of 6 Directors, Out of which Mr. Ramesh Khanna is a Promoter Director and currently designated as Chairman & Managing Director. Mrs. Rashmi Khanna, wife of Mr. Ramesh Khanna is also one of the Promoter Directors and One Mr. Dinesh Negi is a Nominee Director appointed by IKCI Bank, which has provided Banking facilities to Alfa Numerio for ` 250.00 Crore. Rest 3 Directors are professional Directors.

Mr. Rohan Khanna, son of Mr. Ramesh Khanna & Mrs. Rashmi Khanna has just completed his MBA from a premier college in London and returned to India for joining his family business. When Mr. Rohan Khanna was pursuing his MBA, he was caught by Police Authorities of UK in a rave party and was convicted of using drugs and sentenced for simple imprisonment of 9 months. However an appeal is lying pending for conviction before High Court of London and the conviction was stayed pending conclusion of appeal.

Mr. Ramesh Khanna proposed his appointment as an Additional Director in the upcoming Board Meeting and directed the Company Secretary to include the same as one of the agenda item in the Notice of Board Meeting.

In the same Board Meeting, the Quarterly Results of the Alfa Numerio Limited is also to be approved. The notice of the Board Meeting is sent to all the Directors 3 days in advance of the Board Meeting. Total 4 Directors attend the Board Meeting including Mr. Ramesh Khanna, Mrs. Rashmi Khanna, Mr. Dinesh Negi and one Professional Director. The following item inter-alia was discussed and resolved.

Appointment of Mr. Rohan Khanna as Additional Director

Approval of Unaudited Quarterly Results of the Quarter

A Long Term Product Purchase Agreement with a Partnership Firm where Mrs. Rashmi is One of the Partner holding profit sharing ratio of 51%

The Directors present in the meeting also unanimously approved the short notice of the Board Meeting. Subsequent to approval of the Unaudited

Quarterly Results, the same are forwarded to the Auditors of the Company for its Limited Review Report.

It was also brought into the notice that during the discussion of agenda item pertaining to Long Term Product Purchase Agreement, Mr. Ramesh Khanna stepped down from the chairmanship of the Board Meeting and Mr. Dinesh Negi took over the Chairmanship of the Board Meeting.

The Company later on also emailed the Quarterly results to the stock exchange immediately upon conclusion of the Board Meeting.

Background

XYZ Ltd., a publicly listed company based in India, is in the process of appointing a new independent director to comply with Section 149 of the Companies Act, 2013. This section mandates that a certain number of directors must be independent, particularly for listed companies. The appointment process involves the board’s evaluation of potential candidates, ensuring that they meet the criteria for independence as outlined in the Act.

The current board members are:

Mr. Ashok Patil (Chairman)

Ms. Sunita Rao (Managing Director)

Mr. Rakesh Mehra (Non-Executive Director)

Ms. Kavita Desai (Independent Director)

Mr. Vishal Bhatia (CFO)

XYZ Ltd. needs to fill a vacancy created by the recent resignation of one of its independent directors, Mr. Ajay Singh.

The board is considering following three candidates for the role of independent director:

Mr. Anil Saxena

Former CFO of a major tech firm.

No material or pecuniary relationships with XYZ Ltd.

Known for his integrity and financial expertise.

Ms. Pooja Reddy

Former CEO of a competitor company.

Holds a significant amount of shares in XYZ Ltd.

Has a strong background in corporate governance.

Mr. Deepak Shah

Retired partner from a major law firm.

Provides occasional consulting services to XYZ Ltd.

Experienced in regulatory compliance.

Criteria for appointment of an Independent Director

Background

DEF Manufacturing Pvt. Ltd., a mid-sized company in India, has recently encountered allegations of oppression and mismanagement. Some minority shareholders, feeling marginalized and concerned about the company's direction, have filed a petition under Section 241 of the Companies Act, 2013. This section allows shareholders to approach the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) if they believe that the affairs of the company are being conducted in a manner prejudicial to public interest, oppressive to any member(s), or resulting in mismanagement.

Key Stakeholders:

Mr. Rohan Kapoor (Managing Director)

Ms. Shalini Mehta (CFO)

Mr. Karan Verma (Minority Shareholder)

Ms. Ayesha Khan (Independent Director)

Ms. Priya Nair (Legal Counsel)

Issues Raised

Financial Irregularities: Minority shareholders allege that the company's financial statements have been manipulated to hide losses and inflate profits. They claim this has been done to benefit the majority shareholders and to influence the market perception of the company's performance.

Dividend Policies: Mr. Karan Verma and other minority shareholders are upset that the company has not declared dividends for the past two years despite reporting profits. They argue that the majority shareholders, who control the board, are using these profits for personal benefits and reinvestments that disproportionately benefit them.

Mismanagement Allegations: Allegations include misuse of company funds, improper loans to directors, and unauthorized transactions that have not been approved by the board. The minority shareholders claim

these actions are detrimental to the company's interests and violate corporate governance principles.

Exclusion from Decision-Making: Minority shareholders feel excluded from critical decisions, such as major investments and strategic shifts. They allege that the majority shareholders are making decisions that favor their interests without considering the impact on all shareholders.

The minority shareholders of DEF Manufacturing Pvt. Ltd. have filed a petition under Section 241 of the Companies Act, 2013, alleging oppression and mismanagement. The board must address these concerns legally and transparently to maintain corporate governance standards and protect the company's reputation. The NCLT may intervene if the allegations are substantiated, providing remedies to correct the misconduct and protect the interests of all shareholders.

Board Meeting Discussion

At a recent board meeting, the following points were discussed:

Mr. Rohan Kapoor refuted the allegations, stating that all financial practices have been in compliance with the law and that no dividends were declared to reinvest profits for future growth.

Ms. Shalini Mehta presented audited financial statements showing compliance with regulatory standards, but minority shareholders challenged the veracity of these statements.

Ms. Ayesha Khan expressed concern about the impact of these allegations on the company's reputation and suggested an independent investigation.

Ms. Priya Nair advised that the company should respond legally and consider mediation to resolve the conflict.

Good Luck Ltd. is an Indian software company that has been operating for the past 5 years with clients in the USA, UK, and Singapore. The company has recorded foreign exchange earnings of USD 2,000,000 over the previous three financial years.

Sohit Verma is a senior executive at Good Luck Ltd. His son Rohan has been accepted to a foreign University for undergraduate studies with annual tuition fees of USD 80,000 including accommodation. A month later, Sohit's father, who lives with them, requires specialized medical treatment only available in Germany. The hospital in Germany gave an estimated cost of USD 200,000 for the operation. Sohit accompanied his father, for the treatment to Germany and return back to India.

After one month when Sohit’s father recovered, his wife Riya wants to visit her sister in Melbourne for 3 months. Riya transferred USD 20,000 for her stay. The Verma family has also been considering emigrating to New Zealand, which requires them to show funds of at least USD 300,000.

Looking to expand its global footprint, Good Luck Ltd. decided to establish a new office in Taiwan. To facilitate the initial setup, the company allocated USD 200,000 for the purpose and selected Ramesh, a trusted colleague of Sohit, to lead the initiative. On 20th October 2022, Ramesh traveled to Taiwan to begin work on the new office.

In addition to its business ventures, Good Luck Ltd. remains committed to its corporate social responsibilities. As part of this commitment, the company donated USD 100,000 to establish a Chair of Computer Science at XYZ Institute, aiming to contribute to the academic growth of future technologists.

Meanwhile, the company also decided to streamline its assets and focus on its international expansion. It owned a commercial property in Pune, which it resolved to sell. Sohit, was entrusted with overseeing the sale. He hired a professional agent abroad for sale to foreign clientale. The agent successfully sold the property to a business based in the USA, earning a commission of USD 30,000—representing 6% of the property's value.

Through strategic expansion, social contribution, and smart asset management, Good Luck Ltd. continues to build a strong, globally oriented business foundation.

On the basis of the provided facts and figures, answer the following MCQS in the light of the relevant legal provisions under the Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999.

Avon Enterprises is an Indian technology company founded by Mr. Ram Mehta, who returned to India in 2018 after spending 15 years abroad. During his time in the USA, Ram acquired various assets including a property in California worth USD 1.5 million, investments in US equities worth USD 800,000, and a significant art collection valued at USD 300,000. Upon returning to India, he maintained these foreign assets while establishing his new business in Bangalore.

Avon Enterprises is now planning for international expansion. The company has identified a potential partnership with Garnier Inc., a Singapore-based technology firm. As part of this partnership, Avon Enterprises needs to provide a corporate guarantee of USD 5 million to support Garnier's expansion into South-east Asian markets.

Meanwhile, Avon Enterprises has attracted interest from foreign investors:

London Fund LLC wants to invest USD 10 million for a 15% stake in Avon Enterprises with plans to develop commercial complexes in tier-2 Indian cities.

Singapore AG proposes to invest USD 8 million specifically for Avon's residential township development project in Pune.

Emirates Holdings is offering USD 12 million to fund the luxury farmhouse development venture of the company.

On the personal front, Ram's sister, Sunita, is organizing a cultural dance troupe tour to the United States, requiring approximately USD 180,000 in foreign exchange. Additionally, Mr. Ram wishes to gift USD 300,000 to his daughter who is pursuing her education at Canada University.

Ram's father passed away recently, leaving behind a property in Singapore valued at USD 900,000 to Ram who is now considering whether to sell, retain, or transfer the property to his son.

blocks.text: Always passionate about two-wheelers, Indushekhar, supported by his father Mahesh Shekhar, his uncle Chandrashekhar and also by some other close relatives incorporated a company by the name SpeedWheels Limited way back in 1990. Its Registered Office was situated in Lucknow, and it manufactured variety of scooters in the range of 110CC to 160CC and motorcycles from 100CC to 200CC and 400CC. Slowly and gradually, SpeedWheels Limited started exporting its vehicles and in the course of time, it became one of the leading names in the country as well as in European and African countries. In 2020, Indushekhar’s twins Anirudh and Dhanush joined him in managing the flourishing business while Indushekhar’s father Mahesh Shekhar and his uncle Chandrashekhar stepped down as directors due to the advancing age.
The management team of SpeedWheels Limited then comprised as under which continues to be so:

rows: Director (Human Resources and other Miscellaneous Departments)

blocks.text: Though motorcycles are undoubtedly a thrilling way to travel, offering freedom and adventure with every ride but with the entry of young twins of Indushekhar

came new vistas to manufacture sports bikes. The manufacturing of sports bikes would definitely mean a bike to handle sharp turns, high speeds and aggressive riding. Keeping this fact in view both the brothers thoroughly discussed the manufacturing of sports bikes with their father Indushekhar who wholeheartedly accepted the proposal. It was decided that these would be light-weight, aerodynamic and equipped with powerful engines that would give room for rapid acceleration and top speed. This project soon got speed and the demand for sports bikes saw great acceleration in the market.

The overall business was flourishing by leaps and bounds. In continuation, some of the important figures extracted from the audited Balance Sheet of SpeedWheels Limited (as at March 31, 2025) are as under:

rows: Authorised Share Capital (divided into 5 crore equity shares of ` 10 each)
Remaining Term Loan from Star Commercial Bank Ltd.

blocks.text: Seeing good results especially of the new product, the confidence of the twins reached the next level. Dhanush is now desirous of making overseas direct investment (ODI) in Automotive Bikes Manufacturing Co. Ltd. based at United Kingdom (U.K.). In fact, about six months before, he had met Richards, a director in Automotive Bikes Manufacturing Co. Ltd. at Singapore where a Seminar was being held by the Techno Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Singapore. Having similar interests, both Dhanush and Richards developed a strong bond, and it was agreed between them that SpeedWheels Limited would invest a sizeable amount by purchasing fresh equity shares of Automotive Bikes Manufacturing Co. Ltd.

About a month before, SpeedWheels Limited received an advance payment of USD 50,000 from United Motors & Dealers AB of Sweden against supply of 200 sports bikes. This deal culminated because of the vigorous efforts of Divyanshi,
Director (Marketing), who could convince her similar counterpart Elias, Director (Marketing) of United Motors & Dealers regarding the superiority of her company’s product.

Anirudh, Director (Finance and Accounts), had a friend Smith from his college days who after completing his studies in India had shifted to Czech Republic in Central Europe. Anirudh sent him a bottle of ‘Misty Petal Perfume’ as a gift on his first wedding anniversary. For this purpose, he simply made a declaration that the ‘Misty Petal Perfume’ was being exported by way of gift and its value was ` 1,32,000 only. He did not make any declaration in one of the specified forms set out in the Export of Goods and Services Regulations, 2015 as is usually required in the case of export of goods.

For travelling on new roads, SpeedWheels Limited is planning to launch a new assembly facility in Nepal in partnership with Omkar Motors Limited. Dhanush was entrusted to finalise the Agreement between his company and Omkar Motors. However, before finalisation, Dhanush instructed Sambhavie to visit Nepal to take stock of as to how the new assembly facility will function in future. It is worth noting that the proposed facility at Nepal would have a capacity to produce about 50,000 units per year and the said joint venture would also result in creating sizeable job opportunities for the unemployed youth in Nepal. Sambhavie estimated to take USD 4,000 to Nepal for her forthcoming visit and for this purpose she approached her banker National Commercial Bank to withdraw the foreign exchange.

scenario:
In order to boost the production with added quality control features, the Board of Directors of Pure Spices (India) Limited is contemplating to import a sophisticated machinery worth GBP 2,00,000 (falling in the category of capital goods) from SICAL Machineries Ltd. located at London on deferred payment terms. After due negotiations, the agreement with this foreign company was finalised and the machinery is expected to land at Mumbai Port by the end of June, 2025.

Two NRI friends of Avvindra Pratap, namely, Shubindra and Veenu along with their respective wives i.e. Kritika and Shruti, are running SV Fruit Juices & Bottling Plant Limited at Singapore where different kinds of fruit juices are extracted, and their bottling is undertaken. Shubindra and Veenu, being desirous of incorporating a company in India to undertake the same business of extraction of fruit juices and bottling them, requested Avvindra Pratap to join them as director. As initial investment, an inward remittance of SGD 15,00,000 was received in India from Singapore and a company by the name SVKS Fruit Juices and Bottling Plant (India) Limited was incorporated in April, 2025. As

reimbursement of pre-incorporation expenses, Indian entity SVKS Fruit Juices & Bottling Plant (India) Limited remitted SGD 75,000 to SV Fruit Juices and Bottling Plant Limited.

It is not out of place to mention that that Avvindra Pratap, his wife Shvveta (MBA from FMS, Delhi University) and his son Ankkit Pratap (pursuing Engineering Course from Boston University, USA) are partners in a partnership firm, namely, AS Distributors & Co. which is registered under the Indian Partnership Act, 1932. In addition to having total distribution rights of ‘Shuddh Masale’, AS Distributors & Co. also owns distribution rights of at least ten other reputed brands. Ankkit Pratap has suggested his father to make overseas direct investment worth USD 60,000 through their partnership firm AS Distributors & Co. in a USA based online distribution company (located at Boston) which is owned by his four close friends who are citizens of USA. It is proposed by Ankkit Pratap to make investment by way of acquisition of unlisted equity capital of this foreign entity. Avvindra Pratap is seriously considering the proposal whether to make investment through AS Distributors & Co. or not.

To meet financial needs of his son Ankkit Pratap at USA, Avvindra Pratap has made one remittance of USD 80,000 in May, 2025. In the coming month of July, 2025, the parents of Avvindra Pratap want to visit their grandson Ankkit for which Avvindra is contemplating to remit USD 40,000 to his son.

Shvveta is a firm believer in ‘Past Life Regression Therapy (PLRT)’. From time to time she conducts ‘Past Life Regression’ sessions though they are limited to close relatives and friends who have given encouraging views as to the healing through this therapy. In order to equip herself with better and improved knowledge concerning PLRT and to expand her horizon to include foreigners besides Indians, she imported some highly professional books worth Euro 10,000 from John Publishers & Distributors of U.K. where her cousin Revati is holding a senior position. Revati favoured her to make payment of Euro 10,000 at her convenience after the receipt of books.

On 1 August 2025, the NCLT admitted a Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) application filed by Alpha Finance Ltd. (a financial creditor) against MAHA Infrastructures Pvt. Ltd. The petition proposed the name of Mr. Rajesh Singh, a registered Insolvency Professional (IP), to act as Interim Resolution Professional (IRP). No disciplinary proceedings were pending against him.

The NCLT accordingly appointed Mr. Rajesh Singh as IRP on 1 August 2025, which is the insolvency commencement date.

The following facts are relevant:

MAHA’s Board of Directors had passed a board resolution on 15 July 2025 authorizing transfer of ` 60 lakh to its connected subsidiary.

The IRP received multiple claims after making the public announcement and began verifying them.

MAHA has three bank accounts, one each with SB Bank, AB Bank, and HB Bank. All banks awaited instructions on account operation.

Assets worth ` 4 crore are leased, and staff salaries worth ` 25 lakh per month are unpaid.

To continue operations, IRP seeks to raise interim finance of ` 80 lakh but wants to use fixed assets already mortgaged to banks as security.

Committee of Creditors (CoC) is expected to appoint a Resolution Professional (RP) in their first meeting on 25 September 2025.

In the light of the given facts and figures, answer the following Multiple Choice Questions:

Banyan Tree Enterprises Pvt. Ltd., incorporated in India for the past 6 years, has not conducted any business operations or significant financial transactions for the last two consecutive financial years (FY 2023-24 and 2024-25). The company did not file financial statements or annual returns for these two years, stating minimal activity.

Despite inactivity, Banyan Tree continued paying statutory fees to the Registrar of Companies (RoC) and paid rent for maintaining its office and accounting records regularly during the past two years. The directors now plan to apply to the RoC for the company to be declared a dormant company under Section 455 of the Companies Act, 2013, to reduce regulatory compliance requirements.

Additional facts:

There is no pending legal dispute or management conflict.

The company has no loans, outstanding statutory dues, or workmen dues.

However, an investigation was initiated against Banyan Tree six months ago, which is currently in progress but no report has been concluded.

The directors ask whether a special resolution in the general meeting is required before making the dormant status application.

Answer the given questions in the light of the Companies Act, 2013

XYZ Ltd., a technology manufacturing company, plans to raise funds through an Initial Public Offer (IPO). The company files its Draft Red Herring Prospectus (DRHP) with SEBI and the stock exchanges. The key facts are as follows:

XYZ Ltd. has applied for in-principle approval for listing its shares on BSE and NSE, designating NSE as the lead exchange.

It has entered into an agreement with NSDL for dematerialization. However, 10% of promoter shareholding is still in physical form.

The IPO proposes to raise ` 2,000 crore, of which:

` 1,000 crore will be used for project expansion.

` 600 crore is earmarked for investment in a target company (yet to be finalized).

` 300 crore is stated for “general corporate purposes.”

` 100 crore is allocated for debt repayment.

Financially, XYZ Ltd. was earlier a partnership firm until April 2022 and converted into a company in May 2022. Its financial statements for FY 2020–22 of the partnership business were restated in the format prescribed under the Companies Act, 2013 and duly certified by statutory auditors in compliance with accounting standards.
